reason: Response A has the characteristic Claude Haiku register: earnest, comprehensive, evenly-weighted survey prose with safe transitions ("From ancient... to modern"), a tidy thesis-and-summary structure, and a sweeping closing generalization ("one of the most practical and important tools we have"). Response B is too writerly and idiosyncratic — short punchy fragments, an aphorism ("the map is not the territory"), contrarian framing ("Maps also lie"), and stylistic risk-taking that reads like a larger model imitating a distinctive "voice" rather than Haiku's default flat, informative output.
